title: The Demand Side: Policymaker Perspectives on Open Technology Research
content:
By: Nicholas Gates (OpenForum Europe)

As part of developing the Open Technology Research (OTR) shared research agenda, the initiative conducted a series of interviews with policymakers and public sector practitioners across multiple regions and institutional contexts. These ranged widely, from national digital transformation agencies in the Global South to digital ministries in Asia Pacific and Europe to sub-national legislative staff in the United States.

The interviews surfaced a remarkably consistent picture: not of a field that lacks research, but of one that remains underdeveloped in its scope and consistently fails to deliver it in forms that policymakers can use. Why is this important? Well, with big moments yesterday like the release of the European Commission’s “Tech Sovereignty Package”, and its accompanying Open Source Strategy, it’s more important than ever to build the connections between researchers, practitioners, and policymakers necessary to feed evidence into policy-making.
creator: @nicholas
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Got Questions About the OTR Symposium 2026? Join Our Info Sessions on 9 June
content:
By: Laszlo Igneczi and Renata Ávila

Two weeks ago, we launched the Call for Proposals for the Open Technology Research (OTR) Symposium 2026, and OTR also published the first draft of our shared research agenda. The response so far has been encouraging, and so have the questions we have been receiving.

To make sure anyone considering a submission can get the information they need, we’re hosting a set of open info sessions on 9 June, timed to reach researchers, practitioners, and policymakers across every region we work with:

  • 08:00 CEST – Asia Pacific
  • 13:00 CEST – Europe and Africa
  • 18:00 CEST – Americas

    • Each session will walk through what the Symposium is, what we’re looking for in proposals, and how the CFP connects to the priorities in our draft research agenda. There’ll also be an opportunity to ask questions directly about the scope, format, what “Shaping the Open Transition” means in practice, or anything else that’s been giving you pause before hitting submit.
snippet: By: Laszlo Igneczi and Renata Ávila
creator: Open Technology Research
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Three Announcements: OTR Research Agenda, OTR Symposium 2026 Call for Proposals, and Welcoming the Digital Infrastructure Insights Fund
content:
Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Advisor, OpenForum Europe)

Today marks a milestone for Open Technology Research (OTR). We’re publishing two things that belong together: the OTR shared research agenda and the official Call for Proposals for the OTR Symposium 2026, taking place 26–27 October at the University of Barcelona. Additionally, we are happy to officially announce the addition of the Digital Infrastructure Insights Fund (D//F) as a full partner for the OTR.

Open technologies – including open source software, open standards, open hardware, open data, but also the governance frameworks that hold them together – are foundational to modern digital infrastructure. Governments deploy them to reduce vendor dependency and assert digital sovereignty. Public institutions build on them to deliver services at scale. Researchers rely on them to share knowledge and replicate findings.
snippet: Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Advisor, OpenForum Europe)
creator: @nicholas
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Open Technology Research Global Consultations: Key Insights from the Americas
content:
Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)

At 19:00 CET on 19 March 2026, the last of three global OTR consultations took place. This roundtable – with representatives of individuals across the Americas – brought together a notably eclectic group: academics, nonprofit programme evaluators, researchers, civil society, practitioners, and people working on digital public goods. Katie Steen-James of the Open Source Initiative (OSI) facilitated, joined by OSI’s Interim Executive Director Deb Bryant.

The conversation was more intimate than its counterparts earlier in the day – more exploratory, and at times more candid about the practical limits of what can be accomplished. Where the Asia Pacific roundtable surfaced concrete regional priorities, and the Europe and Africa session wrestled with structural and definitional questions, this conversation was more exploratory – at times more candid – about the practical limits of what a research network can and cannot do, and about the specific gaps that participants encounter in their daily work.
snippet: Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)
creator: @nicholas
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Open Technology Research Global Consultations: Key Insights from Europe and Africa
content:
Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)

At 14:00 CET on 19 March 2026 – the same day as the Asia Pacific session – the second of three Open Technology Reserach global consultations brought together a broader and more heterogeneous group: energy system modellers, policy researchers, procurement specialists, civil society advocates, open hardware engineers, and open science practitioners, drawn from across Europe and, to a more limited degree, Africa. The conversation was moderated by Nicholas Gates of OpenForum Europe.

This roundtable was longer, noisier, and at times more contested than its Asia Pacific counterpart. It was a sign of the challenge of building a global and inclusive research agenda in a still-expanding research field with genuine tensions that deserve to be aired. The session covered familiar ground – survey findings, research priorities, infrastructure gaps – but participants also pushed back on the framing itself. Several of the most valuable contributions came not in response to the questions asked but in resistance to them.
snippet: Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)
creator: @nicholas
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Open Technology Research Global Consultations: Key Insights from the Asia Pacific Region
content:
Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)

At 08:00 CET on 19 March 2026, researchers, practitioners, and advocates from the Asia Pacific region joined the first of three global consultations convened by the Open Technology Research initiative. Amongst other locations, participants dialled in from India, Taiwan, and New Zealand for a small but geographically and professionally diverse group discussion, moderated by Renata Avila of the Open Knowledge Foundation.

The purpose of these consultations is to ensure that the research agenda being developed by OTR’s three founding partners reflects the realities, priorities, and gaps that practitioners and researchers are actually encountering in their work. Not a research agenda that looks good on paper, but one that is actionable, diverse and inclusive, grounded in necessary context from across regions, and connected to the policy moments where evidence can make a difference. The Asia Pacific consultation offered some early insights into what that agenda needs to address for that region in particular.
snippet: Author(s): Nicholas Gates (Senior Policy Director, OpenForum Europe)
creator: @nicholas
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Shaping a Shared Research Agenda for Open Technology Research: Join Our Global Consultations on 19 March
content:
Author(s): Laszlo Igneczi, Deborah Bryant, and Renata Ávila

While we are still building out our new website and planning our next symposium, Open Technology Research (OTR) is entering one of its most important phases: shaping a shared research agenda that will guide our collective work over the next two years and beyond.

We want that agenda to reflect the real needs of researchers, practitioners, and policymakers working at the intersection of open technology and the public interest. And that means we need to hear from you.

Open technology – open source software, open standards, open data, open knowledge, open hardware, etc – is increasingly central to debates about digital sovereignty, democratic governance, and public sector modernisation across the globe. Yet, the research landscape remains fragmented. Global actors like public sector institutions, philanthropies, and development agencies alike commission case studies and impact assessments endlessly because of a dearth of evidence on what works and what doesn’t when using them at scale.
snippet: Author(s): Laszlo Igneczi, Deborah Bryant, and Renata Ávila
creator: Open Technology Research
sourceDescription: Building Evidence, Connecting Researchers, Informing Policy

title: Open Technology Research Officially Launches to Strengthen Global Research Community
content:
Brussels, February 2026 – OpenForum Europe (OFE), the Open Source Initiative (OSI), and the Open Knowledge Foundation (OKFN) announced last Friday, 30 January at the EU Open Source Policy Summit the official launch of Open Technology Research (OTR) — a strategic partnership designed to strengthen the global open technology research community and advance evidence-based policymaking around open technologies.

Open technologies — including software, data, and standards — underpin today’s digital infrastructure, powering innovation globally across business, government, and society at large. Yet despite their ubiquity, there remains a significant gap in understanding their full social, political, and economic impact.

Open Technology Research addresses this challenge by creating a sustainable global platform for research, knowledge exchange, and policy engagement. The platform will house two flagship initiatives: the Open Technology Research Network (OTRN) and the Open Technology Research Symposium (OTRS).
